issue_title: Cli help is sometimes wrong about what the default for something is
issue_body:
Because `argparse.ArgumentDefaultsHelpFormatter` really doesn't understand the default behaviour of the program, or the semantics of different values.

A few examples:

```
  --disable-hook-validation
                        Ordinarily the commands specified for --pre-hook
                        /--post-hook/--renew-hook will be checked for
                        validity, to see if the programs being run are in the
                        $PATH, so that mistakes can be caught early, even when
                        the hooks aren't being run just yet. The validation is
                        rather simplistic and fails if you use more advanced
                        shell constructs, so you can use this switch to
                        disable it. (default: True)
```
As far as the user is concerned, the real default is `False` (but argparse doesn't understand its own `store_false` semantics.)

```
  --dialog              Run using interactive dialog menus (default: False)
```
This is a flag that shadows the default behaviour of the code (though it's now deprecated).

```
  -d DOMAIN, --domains DOMAIN, --domain DOMAIN
                        Domain names to apply. For multiple domains you can
                        use multiple -d flags or enter a comma separated list
                        of domains as a parameter. (default: [])

  --expand              If an existing cert covers some subset of the
                        requested names, always expand and replace it with the
                        additional names. (default: False)
```
The real default in these cases is "Ask" :)

```
  -c CONFIG_FILE, --config CONFIG_FILE
                        config file path (default: None)
```
The real default here is loaded from `certbot/constants.py`, and is `/etc/letsencrypt/cli.ini`.

issue_title: SpatialPooler boosting: is called each run, not on dutyCycle only
issue_body:
As I understand it, boosting should happen only at certain times (dutyCycle), this does not happen! 

* `dutyCycle` book-keeping has to happen each iteration, smooth update of `boostFactors` (=boost columns) likely too.
* but boost to columns synapses (`bumpUpWeakColumns()`) should happen only on the update round. 

Doing so does not affect accuracy, improves performance, and imho is "more correct" behavior.

The proto declares a `maxSteps` https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/064f8b1ef003d5ee07405cd5ac41583f83ab1d35/src/nupic/proto/SdrClassifier.capnp#L11

But the Python class never writes to it: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/76e5b07b35764826b34221104e9c43704138b9e3/src/nupic/algorithms/sdr_classifier.py#L406

One acceptable fix would be to remove `maxSteps` from the proto. It's redundant: it's just the maximum element with the `steps`, which is also serialized.

Hi, 
We have been using luigi at Ooyala at a large scale to run thousands of jobs daily. 
We would not like to alert via email for every worker task failure -- only if there are certain number of failures.

After every failure, worker sends an email based on the email configuration settings provided in the luigi.cfg. 
However, scheduler sends an email ONLY after maxing out all the failure attempts.

So, we would like to receive email notifications from scheduler, but not from the worker. Since they read the email configurations from the same section in luigi.cfg, it doesn't look like we can achieve this unless we run scheduler and workers on different machines (where we can configure email for scheduler but not for workers).

**So, here we propose the capability of separate scheduler and worker email settings in luigi.cfg.**

issue_title: setup.py should be more informative on OS X
issue_body:
I've recently been trying to compile cpptraj/pytraj on OS X and ran into an issue that took me some time to track down. After getting Macports/cython etc all set up, I built libcpptraj and then tried to make pytraj. I first got hit with this error:
```
libcpptraj was NOT detected to be installed with openmp. You can recompile it with -openmp flag or
disable openpm install in pytraj by adding --disable-openmp
```
Fair enough - so I went back and re-configured libcpptraj with OpenMP. After that build finished I once again tried to install pytraj. Ended up with a new error:
```
...
8 warnings generated.
clang++ -bundle -undefined dynamic_lookup -L/opt/local/lib -Wl,-headerpad_max_install_names -L/opt/local/lib/db48 build/temp.macosx-10.11-x86_64-2.7/pytraj/c_dict.o -L/Users/test/GitHub/cpptraj/lib/ -lcpptraj -o build/lib.macosx-10.11-x86_64-2.7/pytraj/c_dict.so -O0 -ggdb -fopenmp
ld: library not found for -lgomp
clang: error: linker command failed with exit code 1 (use -v to see invocation)
error: command 'clang++' failed with exit status 1
```
What I found strange was that instead of gcc (my active port) pytraj setup was defaulting to clang. This persisted even when I tried to explicitly set the CC/CXX environment variables. After some digging, I found it was due to this part of setup.py:
```python
if sys.platform == 'darwin':
    # copied from ParmEd
    # You *need* to use clang and clang++ for extensions on a Mac;
    # Anaconda does annoying stuff that breaks this, since their distutils
    # automatically tries to use "gcc", which would conflict with the MacPorts
    # gcc... sigh.
    os.environ['CXX'] = 'clang++'
    os.environ['CC'] = 'clang'
```
So clang is a silent requirement to build on OS X. The problem is that the default clang does not support OpenMP (@swails I think maybe the macports one does?), but requiring OpenMP is the default for pytraj. I think that pytraj's setup.py should be a bit more informative and state that clang is required on OSX, and disable the OpenMP requirement on OS X (with a warning) if the active clang does not support it. @hainm What do you think?

issue_title: Java API Set methods, the return type is to general
issue_body:
In the Java API all Set factory methods from Context return a simple `Expr`. Even `mkSetMembership` or `mkSetSubset`, which should return `BoolExpr`. 

If you now want to use this Expressions as parameters for e.g. `mkAnd` - a Method that expects `BoolExpr` as parameters - you need an explicit down cast. This makes the API usage look unsafe in Java.

I'm currently using wrapper functions that to the casting for me, but I think this should done right in the official API. 
It might also be a good Idea to change all Parameters and return types to `ArraySort` or `SetSort`, but as sets are mostly used in set Expressions this doesn't bother me that much.

The Problem doen't exist in the C API, because there is only a single Expression type.

issue_title: Cached aiohttp.web.Response objects cause requests to hang
issue_body:
## Long story short

<!-- Please describe your problem and why the fix is important. -->
If you use a cached `aiohttp.web.Response` in a middleware function it will cause any subsequent requests to hang.

## Expected behaviour

<!-- What is the behaviour you expect? -->
After thinking for a bit, I suggest that we either:

* Improve documentation, by showing an example of what to do if you wish to cache the response body, or
* Make the response object re-usable between requests. It can still remain unchangeable after `prepare()` has been called, preserving its semantics. However, this is probably not feasible with the current architecture.

## Actual behaviour

<!-- What's actually happening? -->
As far as I understand, `aiohttp.web.Response` is a _Finite State Machine_. Once a `Response`
object is dispatched by the server, it must be emptying some internal buffer. This renders it unusable for subsequent dispatches.

The current solution for avoiding this behaviour seems to be having the middleware generate a new `Response` object, everytime it is called.

## Steps to reproduce

<!-- Please describe steps to reproduce the issue.
     If you have a script that does that please include it here within
     markdown code markup -->
Here is a simple application that reproduces the behaviour:

```python
from aiohttp import web


def my_middleware_factory():

    # this could have been passed in as a default arg, or exist as a global
    state = web.Response(status=200, text='this is cached')

    @web.middleware
    async def middleware(request, handler):
        res = await handler(request)
        return state

    return middleware


async def handler(request):
    return web.Response(status=200, text='Hello World!')


def main():
    app = web.Application()
    app.router.add_get('/', handler)
    app.middlewares.append(my_middleware_factory())

    web.run_app(app)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

```

* When you hit `http://localhost:8080` twice
* Then the second request should hang your http client

## Your environment

<!-- Describe the environment you have that lead to your issue.
     This includes aiohttp version, OS, proxy server and other bits that
     are related to your case. 
     
     IMPORTANT: aiohttp is both server framework and client library.
     For getting rid of confusing please put 'server', 'client' or 'both'
     word here.
     -->
Python 3.6
aiohttp=3.2.1
Ubuntu 16.04
'server'

aiomysql enabled cache?

OS: debian 8.7
python: 3.5.3/3.6.0
aiohttp: 1.3.3
aiomysql: 0.0.9

code:

    async def query(engine, _id):
        async with engine.acquire() as conn:
            async for row in conn.execute('SELECT title FROM tablename WHERE _id = "%s"' % _id):
                print(row)


    async def go(loop=None):
        async with create_engine(user='***', db='***', host='***', password='***', echo=True, loop=loop) as engine:
            await query(engine, 'id')

            await asyncio.sleep(10)
            await query(engine, 'id')


    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(go())


I modified the data in sleep
However, the old value is still returned until the program restarts

We now have a Penman module available (https://github.com/goodmami/penman) for serializing from arbitrary (but connected) triple lists, so it's a good time to add Penman serialization as an option for MRS.

It should be available for DMRS and EDS, particularly, but it could also be added for MRS (it's just an uglier graph). This could be done by giving the Mrs, Dmrs, and Eds classes `to_triples()` and `from_triples()` methods (like `to_dict()` and `from_dict()`). The penman module could then serialize them.

Thus, as the JSON serialization option requires two calls:

```python
json.dumps(Mrs.to_dict(x))
```

The Penman serialization would also require two:

```python
penman.dumps(Mrs.to_triples(x))
```

issue_title: Loopback does not work with asyncio
issue_body: Loopback interface always raises `writeTimeoutError` on any write using asyncio SerialTransport. It happens because asyncio implementation sets timeout to 0 (`aio.py:L54`) and loopback checks if it can write a data in given timeout (obviously it can't).

issue_title: DeepFool attack returns none type while the adversarial images were generated
Leading zeros in ExtendedPrivateKey break PrivateKey
issue_body:
When creating a PrivateKey from the output of serialize_key() on ExtendedPrivateKey the leading zeros that this data has break the pub() function on the PrivateKey. Code example below:

```
rootkey = 'tprv8....'

# this throws an error
PrivateKey(
    ExtendedPrivateKey.decode(rootkey).serialize_key()
).pub()

# this one works
PrivateKey(
    ExtendedPrivateKey.decode(rootkey).key.serialize()
).pub()
```

Clickhouse allows to set an ID on queries, you can see this IDs with a:
```
select query_id from system.processes;
```
this is useful when you want to cancel a query from an external process as explain here: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/40546983/how-to-kill-a-process-query-in-clickhouse

I propose that the `connection.send_query()` method accepts a string argument to name the query. It could be used in `client.process_*_query()` functions and `execute()` as an optional argument:

```
import uuid
c = client('server')
query_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
c.execute("select something", queryid=query_id)
```

issue_title: allow calling CWT with discrete wavelets?
issue_body:
A user on the mailing list asked why Haar and Daubechies wavelets are not allowed for `cwt`.  In Matlab R2012b, the `cwt` does allow using 'haar', 'db4', etc as the wavelet name.  However, in R2016b Matlab apparently [replaced their cwt with a new implementation that operates differently](https://www.mathworks.com/matlabcentral/answers/306841-tell-me-this-is-not-true-r2016b-and-cwt-continuos-wavelet-transform).  Apparently the old cwt is still available as well.  

I can verify that our existing code does actually already give the same coefficients as R2012b Matlab for the Daubechies wavelets if we change the following line:

https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/bf7be1f4293b44a85a34dbce24b4f4573137775d/pywt/_cwt.py#L72

to 
```python
if hasattr(wavelet, 'complex_cwt') and wavelet.complex_cwt
```
so that an AttributeError is avoided.

I am not an expert in CWT analysis, so I don't know if there are strong use cases for these wavelets in a CWT analysis in practice.

I wanted to rename a class method's name by using the EncodedMethod object's set_name member function.  While the change would not be written back to the APK, DAD and Xrefs would see the new name.

I expected the method's name to change, the function to be in the Python namespace with that new name, and to not rename other methods.  Instead, I found the names of methods throughout the APK were changed.

I have a suspicion of what's wrong but I'm not sure how to fix it.  Basically, when you call `set_name`, it sends a message to the `ClassManager` to set the method name (set_hook_method_name), and then reload to pull in the new name.  The CM method changes the name by modifying the entry in the string table and then doing other reloads.

The issue is if you have an obfuscated APK which uses overloading and simple, single letter method names.  In that case, you could have methods throughout the APK with the method name of 'a', 'b', 'c', etc... by setting the entry in the string table and reloading,  In other words, the `MethodIdItem.name_idx` is common to multiple methods, some with possibly entirely different purposes.
Expose a `get_all_namespaces` api command that lists all of the currently registered blockstack namespaces.

issue_title: install_* has weak guarantees of prod-dev parity
issue_body:
I argue version control the `install_*` options in the `marabunta.yml` is not an optimal design choice, as it assumes dev-prod environment parity (actually it assumes parity over the whole git based code life cycle).

Although, this should be the ultimate goal of every developer, this is a very bold and improbable assumption.

While not only the `install_command` might diverge in different deployment scenarios, some deployment models incorporate a standardized [dynamic construction of addon-paths](https://github.com/xoes/dockery-odoo/blob/shared/base/entrypoint.d/10-construct-addons-folder.sh).

This makes it necessary to lift migration options up to the environment, in other words: command line options.

For that reason, I do not even see a lot of sense in keeping those options within the migration spec.
If someone wishes to git life cycle those options a CI/CD environment definition file might be probably a multiple times better place to do so and through the command line / `EnvVariable`

Therefore I'll prepare a PoC PR to alleviate this, in my opinion inhibiting, design decision.

issue_body:
**Contexto**

**Propuesta**

Agregar un argumento opcional `prepend_catalog_identifier = True` que indica si debe o no _prependearse_ el identificador de un catálogo al identificar del dataset que se desea escribir en CKAN.

Since 6.1.13 (and it got worse in 6.1.14) we've been failing on a number of `GangaList` tests which, after some investigation, needs some discussion. The primary bug I've come across is a change in how calling `copy` on a `GangaList` preserves references or not.

If you make a `GangaList` with:
```python
gl = GangaList()
gl.append(LocalFile())
```
then you have a `GangaList` with a single `LocalFile` in it. Calling:
```python
gl2 = copy.copy(gl)
```
should, by normal Python convention, create a new `GangaList` where `id(gl) != id(gl2)` _but_ `id(gl[0]) == id(gl2[0])` so while the outer container has been copied, the contained objects are references to the same thing.

One of the conditions is this (assigning `self._list` in `makeGangaListByRef()`): An object is being assigned to a schema item which is not marked as a `sequence` but the item is a `ComponentItem` and the object we're passing in is a list-type thing (in this case an actual Python `list`). The code will make a deepcopy-type `cloneVal()` of the object, converting any list-type things to `GangaList`s. This has the effect of deepcopying all the objects so that none of the `id`s match anymore.

Can anyone thing of a solid reason why we would want to be doing a recursive deepcopy of a list object when assigning it to a `GangaObject`? Or should I fix the code so that it's back to 6.1.12 when all the tests passed?

issue_title: PDF embedding not working for fonts made via macOS 10.13 Print dialog
issue_body:
We have custom and retail fonts produced using fontmake which exhibit this issue. When on macOS 10.13.3+ if you create a PDF using the Print dialog > Save as PDF (e.g. from TextEdit) and then open this PDF with Adobe Acrobat, the fonts are cannot be found.

We started getting reports about this issue this year, so it might have appeared with some of the recent versions of fontmake.

After some experiments, I think the issue is in CFF top dictionary records. When I remove the FontName record using fontTools (not the CFF attribute), it is all fine. This issue happens for various fonts with standard as well as non-standard weight names, with 4-style (or 2-style) family grouping as well as for a single-style family.

CFF top dictionary in fonts generated with other tools than fontmake (just a quick ttx/look at fonts generated from the same source):

*AFDKO:*

- no FontName
- no FullName
- no Weight

*Glyphs:*

- no FontName
- no FamilyName

Here is the code I am using to fix this:

```
from fontTools.ttLib import TTFont

font = TTFont(path)
if "CFF " in font.keys():
	cff = font["CFF "].cff
	topDictrawDict = cff[cff.keys()[0]].rawDict
	del topDictrawDict["FontName"]
font.save(outpath)
font.close()
```

issue_title: Unplayable tracks in a browse listing cause the incorrect track to be selected
issue_body:

This means that the client side code to play the selected track from the list selects the wrong track as the index doesn't get updated.

The code I changed uses track URI to select the track, rather than incrementing thru a tracklist.

I submitted my change here: https://github.com/colinnuk/mopidy-musicbox-webclient/commit/2eb4141f72ce1ec2ba60b5953ab150d2c9c7b268

But I just realised your develop branch has a rather different function entirely, which could fix the issue?

issue_title: Provide an easy way to preload some packages that exist on pypi.python.org
issue_body:
One of the most important advantages of having a private pypiserver is that you decouple yourself from the dependency of pypi.python.org which can go down or receive changes that may impact you.

We need a documented way to do something like:

* upload latest version of package `foobar` from pypi.python.org to our internal server
* upload foobar==1.2.3 from pypi to our internal server
* remove package version from internal server

Please note that fallback feature cannot be used because it does affect all packages. We need to be able to control what and when gets updated.

The workaround at this moment is to manually download these files from pypi and to put them on disk, which is an ugly manual process.

issue_title: Biased Mean Quantile Returns for Non-Equal Bins
issue_body:
I was doing a study today, and I came across a potential issue with the `performance.mean_return_by_quantile` logic in the returns tear sheet. I guess it is not so much an issue with the logic of that function, but more so how it is used in building the tear sheet. I think this would only be an issue if a user created bins or quantiles with a non-uniform number of observations in each bin over time. In other words, the amount of stocks in each bin changes over time.

If the number of stocks in a bin is correlated with market direction, this could bias the mean return calculation. For instance, if a factor quantile tends to have a lot of stocks when the market is going up, it will receive a higher weight on those up-days and will thus bias the mean return upwards for that factor quantiles. 

[Here is an example I created.](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1vVncTm_TKilb8bYjz5A2BdZ5pkacef-JgJrMApdEzig/edit?usp=sharingl) Note how it affects the mean return of the spread of going long the best quantile and short the worst quantile.

I believe the `mean_return_by_quantile` function already has the ability to group by date, so therefore, you could calculate the mean return for each quantile and date first, and then take the mean by quantile. 

Am I thinking about this right?

issue_title: Simplify output by making it a list of HostOutput
issue_body:
### Feature Request

* Modify output returned by `run_command` to be list of all host output instead of dictionary.

This will allow for more simplified code to iterate through output and alleviate the side effects caused by output being keyed by host, such as having to de-duplicate identical hosts, `join` only affecting hosts that have matching keys in output and clients (which may have been de-duplicated) as well as performance enhancements from output as dictionary taking up a substantial amount of memory for large number of hosts.

Join will also no longer require an output argument, it can be optional.

The library will guarantee that the order in hosts list will be preserved in output list, so users can have 1-to-1 mapping of host to output.

Note - `host_clients` will need to be modified to be keyed by (host, host_num) pairs to account for duplicate hosts, where `host_num` is the index of host in hosts list.

Also, since the number of hosts is known once `run_command` has completed, `array` module with fixed size arrays can be used to further limit memory use.

Example output gathering after this change:

```
output = client.run_command(<..>)
for host_output in output:
    for line in host_output.stdout:
        print(line)
    print("Command finished for %s:%s with %s" % ( 
          host_output.host, host_output.port, host_output.exit_code))
```

Join with no output:

```
client.run_command(<..>)
client.join(consume_output=True)
# Optionally get output of last commands for exit codes
for host_output in client.get_last_output():
    print("Host %s, exit code %s"  % (host_output.host, host_output.exit_code))
```

This is a backwards incompatible API change that will take effect in the next major version release. 

Warning to be added on next point release.

The only breaking change is iterating over host output - without `.values()` - will yield host output instead of host name.

issue_title: Drop extra attribute from OneTo*Field classes
issue_body:
Here, in full, the definition of the `OneToOneField` class:

```python
class OneToOneField(Field):
    """Field that represents a reference to another entity.

    All parameters not documented here are passed to :class:`Field`.

    :param entity: Either a class or the name of a class.
    :param module: A string. A dotted module name. Used by
        :meth:`OneToOneField.gen_value`. See that method for more details.

    """

    def __init__(self, entity, module=None, *args, **kwargs):
        self.entity = entity
        super(OneToOneField, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)

    def gen_value(self):
        """Return the class that this field references."""
        return self.entity
```

What's wrong here? The class requires that a `module` argument be provided, and the argument is stored as an instance attribute. The docstring states that `module` should be "a dotted module name" and that it is used by the `gen_value` method. However, that's not true. In reality, the `module` attribute is absolutely useless. It does nothing. The `module` argument and attribute are outdated relics of when the `OneToOneField` was a class attribute. As a result, it should be removed.

Similar comments apply to `OneToManyField`.

The follow modules are needed for full python 3 support:

- [x] gevent 1.1
- [ ] protobuf 3.0.0

issue_title: How to handle temporary task errors?
issue_body:
How should Ray tasks behave in the face of temporary errors, specific to a worker? If a task raises an Exception, Ray will wrap this in a RayTaskError and never retry this task. This is usually what we want: but if the error is specific to the task running on that worker, this seems undesirable.

I came across a case like this today in the wild. When an AWS spot instance is scheduled for termination, all processes get killed, but -- in some cases -- not before a file share that my tasks depends on is unmounted. Accordingly, when an AWS spot instance is preempted, I get a FileNotFoundError for some percentage of my tasks whereas the rest are (correctly) retried.

Right now I can't see any way for my task to signal to Ray "this is a problem with this node, not the task itself". My workaround is to just kill the worker using sys.exit when this happens, before the exception can be caught by Ray! It then just handles it like a lost worker, which is OK. 

However, it'd be nice to have something like a NodeError exception that the task can raise so we get some reporting for what happened? Alternatively, Ray could automatically retry tasks up to some limit even after receiving an Exception (perhaps controllable as a parameter to ray.remote), although I'm weary of anything that will delay the reporting of errors.
[will fill in later]

I'm trying to understand how the initiation vector (IV) is determined, and what implications that has for security with different encryption algorithms. 

I understand that ChaCha20 uses a 64 bit IV, while AES uses a 128 bit IV.  I understand that IV reuse is catastrophic to security, so it is important not to reuse an IV with the same encryption key.  I also understand that the client and server each use the same encryption key (which is derived from the passphrase) and each therefore have to pick an IV for each connection. 

What I can't figure out from reading the source code is how the IV is determined.   

Is the IV random?  If so, then it would seem that using AES (or some other cipher with a 128 bit IV) is much more secure than using ChaCha20 (or some other cipher with a 64 bit IV), due to the chances of reusing an IV. 

Is the IV not random?  If so, how is the IV picked? 

Perhaps the documentation could explain the IV selection process in more detail.  I would volunteer to write that documentation, once I understand the above information.

issue_title: skimage FTBFS on multiple architectures testsuite failures.
issue_body:
## Description
When building skimage on Debian autobuilders the test suite fails on several architectures.
In the [bug log](https://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=840589#35) it is explained that when checking with gdb it is just by chance that the failure does not happen with amd64.  The problematic commit is d29831882daef9b7d7691becc3669349d642b547.  The variable firstbg is only initialized conditionally in the first loop. It depends on the array values and the and background value. Still it is used as the starting pointer on the second loop, even if uninitialized. At best the pointer point to the wrong address, at worst it generates an out of bound access causing the segmentation fault.

## Way to reproduce
See [bug log](https://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=840589#35)

- [ ] Debian Linux 
- [ ] 0.12.3

issue_title: Should recurse=True be the default, even though it breaks functionality?
issue_body:
In general, it solves a lot of problems (avoids a `NameError` in looking up globals used inside a function) to have `dill.settings['recurse'] = True`  Note that `multiprocess` and `pathos` perform much more robustly on Windows when `dill.settings['recurse'] = True`.  See: https://github.com/uqfoundation/multiprocess/issues/6.

The number of test cases it breaks seem to be few. See #105.

Should `dill.settings['recurse']=True` be promoted to the default -- even before the corner cases in #105 are addressed?

issue_body:
| Required Info  |   |
|---|---|
| Camera Model | R200 | 
| Firmware Version | 1.0.72.10  | 
| Operating System & Version | Windows 7  |
| Build System |  MingW Makefile | 

Hello,
I have a following idea:
RealSense is not officially supported in Window 7 because of old Media Foundation driver. Maybe use libuvc instead? But doesn't UVC needs isochronous transfer ... which is again unsupported by WinUsb in Windows 7? That is true, but if ... 
1. Install libusbK driver for realsense (see Zadig http://zadig.akeo.ie/) - it supports everything needed.
2. Build libusbx that supports isochronous transfer and works with libusbk (https://github.com/JoshBlake/libusbx). Steps 1&2 have been used to solve isotranfer for Kinect ( https://github.com/OpenKinect/libfreenect ) on windows and it worked! 
3. Build librealsense with RS_USE_LIBUVC_BACKEND instead of RS_USE_WMF_BACKEND. 

I have build librealsense using steps 1->2->3 with success. It works ... to a certain point.
- cpp-enumerate-devices example gives all interfaces and modes.
- examples (cpp-tutorial-1-depth or cpp-tutorial-2-streams) device has been found, UVC negotiations for given format is successfull (depth, camera, infrared2). There are two problems:
1. Interfaces are recognized correctly 0-infrared, 2-depth, 4-camera. But then for libusb_claim_interface it is asking for 1-3-5 (interface+1...and the do not exist :( ). I made some changes in realsense/src/libuvc code to overcome this. Then all interfaces are used correctly.
2. Unfortunatelly, even with previous changes, everything hangs in LIBUSB_TRANSFER_STALL. Any ideas? Any suggestions? 
I think this is important for many Windows 7 users which dont want to change to Win10 only for RealSense :)

... and .. please do not answear:
- "it is impossible for windows 7" - everything is possible (see Kinect case :) )
- "do not use MingW, only VisualStudio" - your mother is using VisualStudio, I dont have to :)
- "i have the same problem, without success..." - I know.

issue_title: Endpoints 2.0.0b5 raises exception when loading an _ApiDecorator (instead of a protorpc.remote.Service)
issue_body:
I ran into a bug when trying to add an API to endpoints' WSGI application instead of each protorpc service. The following sample application works in endpoints 1, but not in endpoints 2.0.0b5:

main.py:
```python
import endpoints
import protorpc


test_api = endpoints.api(
    name='test',
    version='v1',
    title='Test API',
    allowed_client_ids=[endpoints.API_EXPLORER_CLIENT_ID],
    audiences=[],
    scopes=[endpoints.EMAIL_SCOPE]
)

@test_api.api_class(resource_name='tests', path='tests')
class TestService(protorpc.remote.Service):
    pass


ENDPOINTS_APPLICATION = endpoints.api_server([test_api])
```

Error when main.py is loaded:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/wsorvis/google-cloud-sdk/platform/google_appengine/google/appengine/runtime/wsgi.py", line 240, in Handle
    handler = _config_handle.add_wsgi_middleware(self._LoadHandler())
  File "/Users/wsorvis/google-cloud-sdk/platform/google_appengine/google/appengine/runtime/wsgi.py", line 299, in _LoadHandler
    handler, path, err = LoadObject(self._handler)
  File "/Users/wsorvis/google-cloud-sdk/platform/google_appengine/google/appengine/runtime/wsgi.py", line 85, in LoadObject
    obj = __import__(path[0])
  File "/Users/wsorvis/src/endpoints2bug/main.py", line 20, in <module>
    ENDPOINTS_APPLICATION = endpoints.api_server([test_api])
  File "/Users/wsorvis/src/endpoints2bug/lib/endpoints/apiserving.py", line 481, in api_server
    apis_app = _ApiServer(api_services, **kwargs)
  File "/Users/wsorvis/src/endpoints2bug/lib/endpoints/apiserving.py", line 239, in __init__
    self.base_paths.add(entry.base_path)
AttributeError: '_ApiServer' object has no attribute 'base_paths'
```

### Root Cause?

`_ApiServer.__init__` in `endpoints/apiserving.py` has a code path that tries to use `self.base_paths` before it is initialized:

```python
  def __init__(self, api_services, **kwargs):
    """... (docstring cut for brevity)"""
    for entry in api_services[:]:
      # pylint: disable=protected-access
      if isinstance(entry, api_config._ApiDecorator):
        api_services.remove(entry)
        api_services.extend(entry.get_api_classes())
        self.base_paths.add(entry.base_path)

    # Record the base paths
    self.base_paths = set()
    for entry in api_services:
      self.base_paths.add(entry.api_info.base_path)

    ...
```

The idea behind excluding packages was to allow users to install wagons in environments where some requirements were already installed (whether of the required dependency version or otherwise). Using constraint files, this feature isn't needed anymore and you can simply put a constraint on the already installed package in your environment.

Thus, the feature of excluding packages should be removed.
It has been in Django since 1.4 and can be converted to String. I can submit a PR but I don't know how you want it. Can it be done the same way as UUIDField?

issue_title: Use mapping for `assets` instead of array
issue_body:
`Registry.sol` currently uses an array to keep track of all registered tokens (https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/441d7637bc89cc7d70c0afa2cc3817b400fe768e/raiden/smart_contracts/Registry.sol#L7). As pointed out in #232 this could be an attack vector, so we should use a mapping instead. 

This is however not as straight forward as it may seem. See #311 and #232 for details on why it is not as simple as it sounds.

issue_title: VCF GT field interpreted as having multiple values
issue_body:
The VCF GT field has multiple values for each of the homologs in the sample, which is represented as multiple numbers with a slash or pipe, e.g. `0/1`. This is parsed into a tuple by `pysam`, for example the following record is converted to the tuple `(0, 1)`:

```
chrM    711 .   G   A   .   REJECT  .   GT:AD:BQ:DP:FA  0/1:1,914:34:915:0.999  0:0,515:.:515:1.00
```

However, by doing this, we are converting a scalar value (`Number=1` in the header) to a vector quantity. If we then try to set this value back to itself, `pysam` will complain we are setting a scalar field to a vector quantity.

For example:

```python
vcf = VariantFile(some_path)
vcf.header.formats.add('EG', 1, 'Integer', 'Some scalar field')
for rec in vcf.fetch():
    gt = rec.samples['TUMOR']['GT']
    rec.samples['TUMOR']['EG'] = gt
```

Will result in the error:

```
    rec.info[key] = value
  File "pysam/libcbcf.pyx", line 2569, in pysam.libcbcf.VariantRecordInfo.__setitem__
  File "pysam/libcbcf.pyx", line 683, in pysam.libcbcf.bcf_info_set_value
  File "pysam/libcbcf.pyx", line 577, in pysam.libcbcf.bcf_check_values
TypeError: value expected to be scalar, given len=1
```

***

For this reason, we probably shouldn't try to split the GT field, and should keep it as a string with slashes. Thoughts?

issue_title: Add unit tests for superset of expected responses.
issue_body:
There was [a bug][] in the Node.js Vision client which was effectively that the client raised an exception if it ever got a key that it did not expect. This is relatively easy to do for any number of reasons:

```python
# `raw_response` is a dict that came back from the API.
key_map = {
    'alphaObject': 'alpha_object',
    'betaObject': 'beta_object',
}
new_response = {}
for k, v in raw_response.iteritems():
    new_response[key_map[k]] = v
```

This is an anti-pattern, because it strongly assumes that you will never get a new key that is not already in your key map. If the API adds a `gammaObject` key and this (Python) code were to run, suddenly what used to work now raises `KeyError`.

The solution: Add unit tests in each spot where we transform an API response into a custom object to ensure that unexpected key-value pairs are either passed through or dropped.

  [a bug]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-node/issues/2034

issue_title: Datastore class leaks http connections, eventually runs out so all requests block & timeout
issue_body:
If a Datastore object is created with DatastoreOptions that include HttpTransport whose HttpClient has set a org.apache.http.impl.conn.PoolingHttpClientConnectionManager, RemoteRpc soon exhausts all available connections.  Subsequent requests to the Datastore object block until the timeout expires and then fail.

The issue I believe is com.google.api.services.datastore.client.RemoteRpc does not call org.apache.http.conn.HttpClientConnectionManager.releaseConnection() to return a connection back to the connection manager and make it reusable by other consumers.

issue_title: Folium not working in Jupyter Notebook with Microsoft Edge (Windows 10, python 3.6.0)
issue_body:
I get nothing but a blank screen area when running the following example from the May 18, 2017 folium.pdf in Jupyter Notebook with Microsoft Edge:

import os
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import folium

map_osm = folium.Map(location=[45.5236, -122.6750])
map_osm.save('osm.html')
map_osm

It works in Firefox, so I guess I'll just use it instead of Edge.  That's not a big deal, but I wanted to pass this info on in case it saves someone else an hour out of their lives in debugging :).
